perturbation.original_text: All representations and warranties made in this Agreement shall survive the Closing of this Agreement, except that any party to whom a representation or warranty has been made in this Agreement shall be deemed to have waived any misrepresentation or breach of representation or warranty of which such party had knowledge prior to Closing. Any party learning of a misrepresentation or breach of representation or warranty under this Agreement shall immediately give written notice thereof to all other parties to this Agreement. The representations and warranties in this Agreement shall terminate one year from the Closing Date, and such representations or warranties shall thereafter be without force or effect, except any claim with respect to which notice has been given to the party to be charged prior to such expiration date.
perturbation.changed_text: All representations and warranties made in this Agreement shall survive the Closing of this Agreement, except that any party to whom a representation or warranty has been made in this Agreement shall be deemed to have waived any misrepresentation or breach of representation or warranty of which such party had knowledge prior to Closing. Any party learning of a misrepresentation or breach of representation or warranty under this Agreement shall immediately give written notice thereof to all other parties to this Agreement. The representations and warranties in this Agreement shall terminate six months from the Closing Date, and such representations or warranties shall thereafter be without force or effect, except any claim with respect to which notice has been given to the party to be charged prior to such expiration date.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the survival period for representations and warranties to six months is unduly short and may conflict with statutes of limitations for breach of contract claims, which are generally longer in Nevada.
perturbation.law_explanation: Nevada Revised Statutes § 11.190 sets a statute of limitations for contract claims. A six-month survival period for representations and warranties may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable if it prevents a party from pursuing a claim within the statutory timeframe. Nevada law allows for a longer period to bring a claim, and this change reduces the time drastically.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the survival period for representations and warranties from one year to six months. The Law Explanation states that Nevada Revised Statutes § 11.190 sets a statute of limitations for contract claims, and a six-month survival period may be deemed unreasonable if it prevents a party from pursuing a claim within the statutory timeframe. The Perturbation Explanation further notes that reducing the survival period may conflict with statutes of limitations for breach of contract claims. Scraped Law Snippet 2, NRS: CHAPTER 11, confirms the existence of a statute of limitations. The modification directly contradicts the purpose of the statute of limitations by potentially preventing parties from pursuing valid claims within the legally prescribed time.

perturbation.original_text: All notices, requests, demands, and other communications required or permitted hereunder will be in writing and will be deemed to have been duly given when delivered by hand, by overnight courier, or fax, or two days after being mailed by certified or registered mail, return receipt requested, with postage prepaid.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices, requests, demands, and other communications required or permitted hereunder will be in writing and will be deemed to have been duly given when delivered by hand, by overnight courier, or fax, or immediately after being mailed by certified or registered mail, return receipt requested, with postage prepaid.
perturbation.explanation: This modification states that notice is deemed given immediately after mailing, which may conflict with legal requirements for reasonable notice and opportunity to respond.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law Principles - Reasonableness of Notice
perturbation.law_explanation: The principle of 'reasonableness' in contract law implies that the recipient of a notice must have a fair opportunity to receive and understand the notice. Deeming notice as given immediately upon mailing eliminates this opportunity, particularly considering potential delays in postal service and processing. Although not a specific statute, this conflicts with established legal precedent regarding the requirements of reasonable notice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: notice | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you notice Notice is the constitutional requirement that a party being brought into court be informed of the controversy . Due Process clauses of the United States Constitution prohibit courts from hearing a case that could adversely affect a party's interest unless that party has been given proper notice. To satisfy this notice requirement, notice must be reasonably calculated, under the circumstances, to inform all interested parties that a lawsuit is pending and that it could adversely affect their interests. The degree of required notice varies depending on what type of jurisdiction a court intends to exercise. To exercise in personam jurisdiction, in-hand service of process is usually required. For in rem actions, which can affect the interests of anyone in the world, the plaintiff usually must inform all known parties of interest by a reasonably reliable means, and may then inform the rest of the world by "notice by publication" - purchasing a notice in a local newspaper multiple times over a period of several weeks. In tricky cases, courts work with plaintiffs to identify the best way of meeting notice requirements. In some unusual circumstances, a court may find traditional methods of notice are not satisfactory for a particular party. Notice is not a mere formality, as the Supreme Court recently discussed in Jones v. Flowers . In Jones v. Flowers , a state seized and sold property for unpaid property taxes. A certified letter meant to notify the owner of the impending sale was returned to the state as "unclaimed." Afterwards, the state made no additional attempt to contact the owner directly. The Court rejected the state's notice as insufficient, emphasizing that notice “must be such as one desirous of actually informing the absentee might reasonably adopt to accomplish it." Because of the constitutional importance of ensuring proper notice, courts will not forgive notice achieved through improper means even where a party actually learns of the controversy. [Last reviewed in October of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex CIVICS the Constitution THE LEGAL PROCESS wex articles civil procedure constitutional law courts and procedure individual rights Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes when a notice is deemed to be given, from 'two days after being mailed' to 'immediately after being mailed'. The Perturbation Explanation states this may conflict with legal requirements for reasonable notice and opportunity to respond. The Law Explanation states the recipient of a notice must have a fair opportunity to receive and understand the notice, however, the modified version eliminates that opportunity. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'notice must be reasonably calculated, under the circumstances, to inform all interested parties that a lawsuit is pending and that it could adversely affect their interests' and emphasizes that notice must be such as one desirous of actually informing the absentee might reasonably adopt to accomplish it. The original contract language allowed for mail delays. The changed contract language does not. The changed language does not provide a reasonable opportunity to respond, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party will pay its or his own legal fees and other expenses in connection with the preparation of this Agreement and the sale of Assets in accordance with this Agreement. However, if any legal action or other proceeding is brought for the enforcement of this Agreement, or because or arising out of an alleged dispute, breach, default or misrepresentation in connection with any of the provisions of this Agreement, the prevailing party will be entitled to recover reasonable attorney's fees and other costs incurred in that action or proceeding, in addition to any other relief to which it or he may be entitled.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party will pay its or his own legal fees and other expenses in connection with the preparation of this Agreement and the sale of Assets in accordance with this Agreement. However, if any legal action or other proceeding is brought for the enforcement of this Agreement, or because or arising out of an alleged dispute, breach, default or misrepresentation in connection with any of the provisions of this Agreement, the prevailing party will not be entitled to recover reasonable attorney's fees and other costs incurred in that action or proceeding, in addition to any other relief to which it or he may be entitled.
perturbation.explanation: Eliminating the prevailing party's right to recover attorney's fees may conflict with Nevada law if the underlying claim involves certain statutory violations where attorney's fees are mandated.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 18.010 allows the court to award attorney's fees to the prevailing party in certain contract cases. This modification restricts such award regardless of whether the Nevada statute allows attorney's fees, thus contradicting the provision of the state law.

perturbation.justification: The original text allows the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees, while the changed text explicitly denies this right. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this conflicts with Nevada law if the underlying claim involves statutory violations where attorney's fees are mandated. The Law Explanation states that NRS 18.010 allows courts to award attorney's fees in certain contract cases, and the modification contradicts this provision of state law. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a login page, the Perturbation Explanation and the Law Explanation together indicate that the change introduces a legal contradiction, falling under 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' where the change violates state law.
